title: Unit 29 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά)

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 29: Diminutives and Augmentatives. This is one of my favorite topics because it’s where the Greek language shows its heart. Suffixes that change the size—and the mood—of a word. I'm William, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... why do Greeks love shrinking things so much?
In Greek, a 'Diminutive' isn't just about something being small. It’s about
to a word, we are making it cute, familiar, and friendly. It’s like a verbal hug.
A verbal hug... I love that. Let's start with the king of diminutives:
It’s Neuter, and it works with almost everything. Athina, give us some examples.
Παιδί – παιδάκι. Σπίτι – σπιτάκι. Νερό – νεράκι.
Child, house, water. It makes the object sound cozy and approachable. You’ll hear people ask for a
at a cafe just to be polite!
It’s very common in social interaction. But we also have feminine diminutives, like
Mommy, little sister, little Maria. It’s an instant way to show closeness.
Now... let's go the other way. The Augmentatives. These make things HUGE. Athina, what’s the suffix for a giant?
It’s feminine even if the word was masculine!
A huge house. Big, beautiful eyes. If you see a great goal in soccer, we say
Pro Tip: Use diminutives freely to sound friendly and warm. Use augmentatives sparingly to express excitement or awe. It’s the soul of informal conversation.
Μπράβο σας! Τα λέμε στο επόμενο μάθημα. Γεια σας!
